response:
Here’s a side-by-side comparison of TCP vs UDP in a plain-text table:

Feature               TCP                                              UDP  
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  
Full Name             Transmission Control Protocol                    User Datagram Protocol  
Connection Type       Connection-oriented                              Connectionless  
Reliability           Reliable (ACKs, retransmissions)                  Unreliable (no ACKs or retries)  
Data Transfer         Stream-oriented                                   Message-oriented (datagrams)  
Flow Control          Yes (sliding window)                              No  
Congestion Control    Yes                                                No  
Handshake             3-way handshake                                   None  
Ordering               Guaranteed, in-order delivery                    No ordering guarantee  
Error Checking        Checksum + error recovery                         Checksum only  
Header Size           20 bytes minimum                                   8 bytes  
Overhead              Higher (more state & control info)                Lower  
Speed                 Slower (due to ACKs, retransmits)                 Faster (minimal protocol logic)  
Typical Use Cases     Web (HTTP/HTTPS), FTP, SMTP, SSH                   DNS queries, DHCP, VoIP, video streaming, online gaming  

Key takeaways:  
- TCP adds reliability, ordering, flow- and congestion-control at the cost of extra overhead and latency.  
- UDP is lightweight and fast, but leaves error recovery and ordering up to the application.
